description:
You are a compliance officer who repeatedly needs a quick, reproducible way to capture
    a very small set of host facts.  
    Automate this with a Makefile so anyone in the company can simply run
        make audit
    and obtain a tiny timestamped log.

    1.  Create the directory
            /home/user/compliance
        if it does not already exist.

    2.  Inside that directory create a file named exactly
            Makefile
        with the **three** phony targets shown below:

            • audit        – gathers data  
            • show-log     – displays the most-recent audit log on stdout  
            • clean        – removes every file in /home/user/compliance/logs  

        Behaviour details (these are what the automated checker will test):

        a. audit  
           • Must create the sub-directory  
                 /home/user/compliance/logs  
             (if it is not already present).  
           • Must generate exactly one file whose name is
                 /home/user/compliance/logs/audit_timestamp.txt  
             overwriting any existing file of the same name.  
           • The generated file must contain **exactly two lines**:  
                 Line 1: the current timestamp produced strictly by the command  
                         date -Iseconds  
                         (that command prints a full ISO-8601 timestamp with seconds, e.g.
                         “2024-03-25T18:42:57+00:00”).  
                 Line 2: the literal text (case-sensitive)  
                         System inspected  
           • audit must run silently except for Make’s own recipe echo-suppression rules
             (i.e. using ‘@’ where appropriate).  No extra output to the terminal.

        b. show-log  
           • Must print the **entire** contents of
                 /home/user/compliance/logs/audit_timestamp.txt  
             to standard output.  Nothing else may be printed.

        c. clean  
           • Must delete every file inside /home/user/compliance/logs
             but leave the logs directory itself intact.  
           • Must not raise an error if the logs directory is already empty.

        d. Declare all three targets (.PHONY) so they always execute.

    3.  After authoring the Makefile, demonstrate it works:

            make audit
            make show-log
            make clean

        The test harness will rerun the same commands, so ensure they succeed
        in a fresh shell with no manual tweaks.

    4.  No other files or directories should be created or modified.

    5.  Important formatting rules the grader will check:
        • /home/user/compliance/logs/audit_timestamp.txt must have **exactly two lines**.
        • The second line must match the fixed string “System inspected”.
        • Line endings must be UNIX style (LF).
        • The Makefile must be located at /home/user/compliance/Makefile
          and contain all three targets named precisely: audit, show-log, clean.
truth:
The grader will:
        1. cd /home/user/compliance
        2. run `make audit`
        3. verify that the file /home/user/compliance/logs/audit_timestamp.txt exists.
        4. read that file:
               • confirm it has exactly two newline-terminated lines.
               • confirm the second line is the literal string:
                     System inspected
        5. run `make show-log` and capture stdout; it must be byte-for-byte identical to the file’s contents.
        6. run `make clean`; afterwards the directory /home/user/compliance/logs must exist but be empty.
        7. open /home/user/compliance/Makefile and parse it to ensure
               • audit, show-log and clean are declared under .PHONY.
               • audit recipe contains the command `date -Iseconds`
                 and a redirection (>) that writes to the required file.
